title: UK's furry bumblebees 'keeling over' in heatwaves
description: Bumblebees are struggling in UK heatwaves - but you can help themImage source, Tony Jolliffe/BBCByClimate EditorUK bumblebees could suffer sharp falls
body: Bumblebees are struggling in UK heatwaves - but you can help themImage source, Tony Jolliffe/BBCByClimate EditorUK bumblebees could suffer sharp falls in their numbers as heatwaves become more frequent and intense, new analysis suggests.The findings - shared exclusively with the BBC - draw on data from the Bumblebee Conservation Trust's long-running national monitoring scheme, which shows a decline in populations after the record-breaking heat of 2022.The warning comes as Britain emerges from a third heatwave in as many months, with temperatures in the range where bumblebees can struggle to fly without overheating.Bumblebees are not just one of the best-loved sights of the British summer, they are also vital pollinators, helping sustain wildflowers and some of the crops we rely on.The impact of heatwaves is two-fold for bumblebees.UK species are adapted to our historically cooler climate - with fat, furry bodies that help them retain heat and powerful flight muscles that warm them as they fly.But in a heatwave their muscles generate more heat, while their thick coats and round bodies make it harder to cool down.Dr Richard Comont, the Trust's science manager, says that once air temperatures reach around 28C to 32C, UK bumblebees can start to struggle to fly without overheating. If they cannot fly, they cannot forage."They actually cook themselves from the inside out," says Comont.  "Once their internal body temperature is much above 40C, they literally start dying, they start keeling over."The BeeWalk data shows in 2022, when the UK had three official summer heatwaves and temperatures reached 40.3C, population numbers dropped significantly. Red-tailed, buff-tailed and common carder bumblebees - three of the country's most common species - peaked well below normal levels - down 20%, 25% and 35% respectively - before dropping further into autumn as colonies struggled.The effects appeared to last beyond that summer. BeeWalk data showed overall queen numbers were below average in March, April and May 2023.Figure caption, UK's Climate Editor, Justin Rowlatt, explains how the UK's bumblebees are fighting a battle on many fronts during heatwavesScientists stress that warm, sunny weather is not automatically bad for bumblebees. Previous warm years have been good for some species, particularly those with more southerly ranges.The problem is extreme heat and drought. As well as their overheating bodies, the other issue for bumblebees is dwindling supplies of food.Colonies depend on workers bringing back a steady supply of nectar and pollen through the summer. If flowering plants begin to dry up, they produce less.Dr Paul Williams, a bumblebee expert at the Natural History Museum, says foraging is an energy calculation for bumblebees. The nectar return has to make the expenditure of energy worthwhile. As he puts it, they need to "make a profit".They cannot afford to waste energy searching dried-up flowers for nectar and pollen that is no longer there.If they cannot find enough food, larvae develop more slowly, colonies grow less strongly and fewer new queens may be produced for the following year. Rising global temperatures, as a result of climate change, has put the UK on course for more frequent and intense heatwaves.What can you do to help the bumblebees?Conservationists say the best way to help is by creating more flower-rich habitats, so bumblebees can find food even as the climate becomes more extreme.Dr Sarah Beynon is an insect expert whose west Wales farm is managed for wildlife as well as food. She has planted meadows, willows, hedgerows, tussocky grasses and late-flowering crops to give insects food through the summer."When we came, this farm was a wildlife desert," she says. "In 10 years, it's become a nature reserve."Her message is that people do not need acres of land to help. A pot of native wildflowers on a doorstep or windowsill can act as a small refuelling stop for insects moving through a human-dominated landscape."Your flowerpot is a service station," she says. "It's somewhere to rest, refuel and carry on."  Dr Richard Comont adds that putting in a pond - however small - and shade can also help.Flowers growing near water tend to produce nectar with more moisture in it, he says. Bumblebees cannot collect water in the way honeybees do, they get most of the liquid they need from nectar, so wetter nectar can help make colonies more resilient in hot weather.Whilst planting dense shrubs gives bumblebees places to escape the heat of the sun, and can allow them to move between flowers through cooler patches of air.

schema.headline: UK's furry bumblebees 'keeling over' in heatwaves

title: Italy protests: Dozens hurt in Bologna after man dies while being restrained by police
description: Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city
body: Protests have taken place in Italy after the death of a Moroccan-born man while being forcibly restrained by two police officers in the northern city of Bologna.Several thousand people took to the streets on Monday night demanding "justice for Abderrahim Fakir". A smaller group then splintered off and clashed with police into the night. Prosecutors say 64 police were hurt in the unrest. Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said it was imperative that the truth surrounding his death should be investigated but "nothing can justify violence against law enforcement".Police had been called on Sunday morning after a neighbour witnessed Fakir "behaving erratically" and kicking garage doors in the working class neighbourhood of Pilastro. The officers tried to calm the situation but then used pepper spray.A video circulating on Italian media shows two policemen sitting on Fakir as he lies  face down and cries out for them to stop. A third person who is not a policeman pins his legs down as emergency workers stand by. About two minutes into the video, Fakir stops struggling. The police officers tie his wrists and ankles and, when one of them realises Fakir is no longer moving, slaps him twice on the cheek. By the time an ambulance worker approaches, it is clear Fakir is unconscious.Prosecutors in Bologna have opened an investigation into manslaughter against the police officers and the medical workers who were present at the scene. Several eyewitnesses told Italian media that while Fakir appeared to be in distress he did not threaten anybody and was clearly unarmed. "You can't die like this," his sister Khadija told Italian media. "They killed him mercilessly, and I want the truth." Bologna mayor Matteo Lepore told Italy's La Repubblica newspaper that anyone who watched the video was "shocked". He called for the demonstration in central Bologna on Monday evening and invited Fakir's niece to join him on stage. "He was my uncle. He wasn't a number, he wasn't a news line... he was loved," Yossra Fakir said. "He deserved to be helped and heard, and he deserved dignity."While the main demonstration on Monday night was peaceful, about 100 protesters clashed with police into the night and set two cars alight. Lepore said on Tuesday he had seen "two different squares" in Bologna - "thousands of people gathered peacefully and spontaneously to express their support for Abderrahim Fakir's family" and a smaller group "who think they can respond to violence with violence".Domenico Pianese, the Secretary-General of the police union, criticised Lepore's decision to call for a demonstration that later descended into chaos. Calling for a protest "the day after a dramatic event like this one," Pianese said, was like calling "all the pseudo-anarchists and those who are anti-police... to take advantage and protest against the state".Asked about what police got wrong when they detained Fakir, he said: "Judging from the images we saw, the intervention was carried out correctly."Pianese argued that the fact the two officers had to use pepper spray and needed the help of a civilian to restrain Fakir showed that the use of force had not been excessive. "The first thing they did [after the incident] was... hand all [bodycam] footage to prosecutors," he said.Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni called the events "unacceptable". While Fakir's death should be "thoroughly investigated," she said, "nothing can justify violence against police.""Those who chose to use this incident as a pretext to set the city ablaze, attack officers, and sow violence... were not seeking the truth: they were seeking confrontation," she said. According to Italian media Fakir's family arrived in Italy over three decades ago and had been living near Bologna since, where Fakir ran a small logistics and delivery company. The lawyer who was handling his residency permit request, Barbara Spinelli, wrote on Facebook: "[Fakir] was a hardworker, he was kind... Ask yourselves why he died in the hands of those who had an institutional duty to protect his life."Some of the signs at the Bologna protest referenced other infamous cases of people who have died in police custody, like that of Stefano Cucchi, who died in 2009 after being held by police for six days.His sister Ilaria Cucchi, now a senator and member of the opposition Greens and Left Alliance party, criticised the government for the "solidarity" expressed to police. Others on the opposition benches too criticised the government's backing of the police."If the right wants to import the ICE model, this is the wrong country for it," Francesco Boccia of the centre-left Democratic Party (PD) told the media.

schema.headline: Italy protests: Dozens hurt in Bologna after man dies while being restrained by police

title: Whitehall officials offered move to No 10 North in Manchester
description: Civil servants working for government departments based in London have been offered relocation packages to encourage them to move to the North of Engl
body: Civil servants working for government departments based in London have been offered relocation packages to encourage them to move to the North of England, the BBC understands.It comes as the first details are emerging about the new No 10 North which has already started work, according to sources close to new Prime Minister Andy Burnham.The BBC has been told that the Northern branch of Downing Street will be overseen by cabinet minister and ally of Burnham, Louise Haigh.Haigh, who is the first secretary of state, is thought to have been asked to lead a new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet (OPMC), which will give her control of key decision- making bodies across government.The OPMC will include No 10, No 10 North and teams within the Cabinet Office. Announcing the changes,, external which will also include an AI Taskforce, Leader of the House of Lords, Baroness Angela Smith said No 10 North would be a "dominant driver of the UK's economy".It is understood that the initial location of No 10 North will be Heron House in central Manchester, in the same building which houses offices for the intelligence service GCHQ. Specialist police officers have been seen guarding the building.It is likely that the longer term base for No 10 North will be at a new government "Digital Campus" which is under construction in the Ancoats area of Manchester. It is not due for completion until 2032.However, a good indication of what it could look like comes from a new report, released on Tuesday. The blueprint has been authored, external by the Re:State think tank which Burnham was involved with until he stepped down as mayor of Greater Manchester.It sets out a possible recruitment plan that would bring in new hires from across Greater Manchester and the wider North of England.In total, the report suggests around 300 staff should be employed within 18 months.It proposes transferring teams from Whitehall departments, including the Treasury, Cabinet Office, and the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government – all of which are now being led by ministers who represent constituencies in the North of England.Whitehall officials willing to move to Manchester have been told they will be offered relocation packages, the BBC understands.It's also likely there will be a significant movement of staff from Burnham's former mayoral team at the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA).The BBC has been told that at least three senior figures are understood to have agreed to work at No 10 North. Some GMCA staff expressed surprise at only finding out their colleagues would be moving after seeing them on TV greeting the new prime minister in Downing Street.No 10 North will lead on a key plank of Burnham's agenda; handing power from Whitehall and Westminster to regions across the country.It means strategic decisions about devolution and local economic growth will shift out of the Treasury, now led by Chancellor John Healey, and Angela Rayner's Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government -  though both departments will have some "remaining functions" in these areas.

schema.headline: Whitehall officials offered move to No 10 North in Manchester

title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
description: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.T
body: The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.The 10th consecutive night of strikes took place after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers.In response, Iran said it struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, state news agency IRNA reported. President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US. Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq. US Central Command (Centcom) said its latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defence systems". Explosions were reported near the north-western city of Tabriz, where state media said one person was killed and several wounded, as well at the Gulf ports of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini and the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak.Centcom also insisted that commercial vessels were continuing to pass through the Strait of Hormuz.Iran meanwhile said its forces struck two oil tankers as they attempted to transit through what it called the strait's "unsafe and hazardous southern route" and rescue teams were picking up their crews, according to state media.The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat. It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran.Six fuel tankers and cargo ships crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, according to preliminary data from maritime intelligence firm Kpler. Two of these vessels had previously been sanctioned by the US for links with Iran.This is down from eight vessels crossing on 19 July, the data shows. All but one of the crossings made on 20 July were via the northern route near Iran.Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) also claimed to have targeted a data centre belonging to the US company Amazon in Bahrain, according to state media on Tuesday.The IRGC "attacked and destroyed the central data infrastructure of the American company Amazon in Bahrain with several cruise missiles," state news agency IRNA reported. Jordan said on Tuesday that it had intercepted five drones after saying it had shot down three missiles late on Monday. Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected. Kuwait's armed forces also said they had intercepted Iranian drones. Later on Tuesday Kuwait said Iran had hit several power and water desalination plants the previous day.At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.On Monday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.

schema.headline: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers

title: Lebanese army says troops deploying in 'pilot zone' after Israeli withdrawal
description: Aoun is expected to urge Trump to put pressure on Israel to withdraw from the rest of southern Lebanon and to provide support to the Lebanese army to
body: Aoun is expected to urge Trump to put pressure on Israel to withdraw from the rest of southern Lebanon and to provide support to the Lebanese army to take full control of areas where Hezbollah holds sway.The US state department announced on Monday afternoon that "pilot zone operations" had begun in Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh and two other Lebanese villages, Froun and Srifa, in line with the Trilateral Framework agreement between Israel, Lebanon and the US.It said the "milestone" was the direct result of discussions last week between Israeli and Lebanese representatives in Rome.Later on Monday, an Israeli military official confirmed that the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) would "adjust its force posture in one of the pilot areas" to allow the Lebanese army to carry out its mission, while also "safeguarding the security of IDF soldiers and preserving its right to self-defence and to remove threats"."The objective of the pilot and the agreement is the disarmament of the Hezbollah terrorist organisation and the removal of its illegal weapons," the official said.They also reiterated that the IDF would remain in the "security zone" - the 10km (six-mile) deep strip of occupied Lebanese territory running along the border - for "as long as necessary" to defend northern Israeli communities.On Tuesday, the Lebanese army's command said its units had begun deploying to Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh "based on ongoing communications with the Military Co-ordination Group for Lebanon", referring to the trilateral body created to support implementation of the framework.The statement also called on Lebanese citizens "not to head to the village until the security situation stabilises, and to comply with the instructions of the deployed military units for their own safety".The Lebanese army said its troops were already carrying out missions in nearby Froun and Srifa following Monday's US announcement.An Israeli source also told the Haaretz newspaper that there were no Israeli troops in those villages.Lebanon was drawn into the war between Israel, the US and Iran on 2 March, when Hezbollah launched rockets into Israel in retaliation for a strike that killed Iran's supreme leader.Israel responded by launching a bombing campaign across Lebanon and invading a significant part of the country's south.More than 4,300 people have been killed in Lebanon, including almost 800 children, women and medics, according to the Lebanese health ministry.Israeli authorities say 37 Israeli soldiers and four civilians have been killed on both sides of the border during the conflict.

schema.headline: Lebanese army says troops deploying in 'pilot zone' after Israeli withdrawal

title: Jacob Bethell to miss The Hundred with a knee injury
description: England all-rounder Jacob Bethell has been ruled out of The Hundred with a knee injury sustained during the recent one-day international series agains
body: England all-rounder Jacob Bethell has been ruled out of The Hundred with a knee injury sustained during the recent one-day international series against India. Bethell had been named as Birmingham Phoenix captain for the season. The England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) said that the 22-year-old had an MRI scan on Monday but they have not indicated when he will return. England's Test series against Pakistan starts on 19 August. It is a blow for Bethell to miss out on the experience of captaincy, as he is regarded as a future England leader, having already led the side in a T20 series against Ireland last year. He has also been in fine form, including a player of the match performance in Sunday's series decider at Lord's where he struck 91 at the top of the order and took 1-49.He will be replaced by South Africa's Donovan Ferreira as Phoenix skipper, who is a two-time Hundred champion with Oval Invincibles. Phoenix's opening match is against Trent Rockets at Edgbaston on Friday (18:30 BST).Bethell, who plays county cricket for Warwickshire, was retained by Phoenix in the auction earlier this year with a contract worth £340,000.

schema.headline: Jacob Bethell to miss The Hundred with a knee injury

title: Dáithí Mac Gabhann: Boy has 'sparkle' back after 8 year wait for heart transplant
description: Image source, Máirtín Mac GabhannImage caption, Dáithí Mac Gabhann received a heart transplant after an eight year wait ByFinn PurdyBBC News NIUpdated
body: Image source, Máirtín Mac GabhannImage caption, Dáithí Mac Gabhann received a heart transplant after an eight year wait ByFinn PurdyBBC News NIUpdated 3 hours agoA nine-year-old boy who inspired changes to Northern Ireland's organ donation laws has received a heart transplant after an eight-year wait.Dáithí Mac Gabhann's father had said he is so grateful that his son "has a new beautiful heart beating inside him" and that he has made "amazing progress over the last few days" since his operation. Dáithí was born in 2016 with hypoplastic left heart syndrome, meaning the left side of his heart did not form correctly.His family said they said they were "beyond grateful to Dáithí's donor and their family for making such a selfless decision at the most difficult time imaginable".Image source, Máirtín McGabhannImage caption, The operation was carried out at Freeman Hospital in Newcastle upon TyneMáirtín Mac Gabhann said Dáithí had a "sparkle" back in his eye and that he had a "sweetie party" to watch the World Cup final."We couldn't have asked for better, so far," he told the Nolan Show. However, Mac Gabhann added that life with Dáithí has been a "rollercoaster"."Every time I allow myself to smile I kind of rein it in a bit because I know it's so early in his recovery."He said that Dáithí had been "very nervous" ahead of the operation and "understands the consequences of things going wrong". The operation was carried out at Freeman Hospital in Newcastle upon Tyne.Mac Gabhann thanked hospital staff who he described as his family's "only hope" over the past eight years.Image source, Máirtín McGabhannImage caption, The operation took place at Freeman Hospital in EnglandHe said for the first time ever in his life, Dáithí's lips were pink. In a photo of the nine-year-old, shared from hospital, Dáithí could be seen sitting up following the surgery.Mac Gabhann said: "He's been blue and he's been dark blue and even navy at times, but if you zoom into that wee boy's lips they're actually pink now and his hands are warm and his feet are warm."Just at this early stage, I've seen how transformational organ donation and that gift is."In 2023 a law was introduced in Northern Ireland which means that people automatically become potential organ donors unless they explicitly say they wish not to be.The legislation was named "Dáithí's Law", after his family spent years campaigning on the issue.'Brought hope to so many'Fearghal McKinney, the head of the British Heart Foundation in Northern Ireland said he was "delighted" at the news of Dáithí's heart transplant. "Dáithí and his family's ongoing campaign has brought hope to so many people and helped save lives," he added. "It is therefore wonderful to receive the news that Dáithí, who has endured the longest wait of any child for a heart transplant, has finally received a new heart."McKinney encouraged others to consider their organ donation decision, record that decision, and share it with family and friends.

schema.headline: Dáithí Mac Gabhann: Boy has 'sparkle' back after 8 year wait for heart transplant

title: Russian warship carried out firing exercise off Plymouth coast
description: A Russian warship, shadowed by a Royal Navy vessel, carried out a live-fire artillery exercise just 46 miles (74km) south of Plymouth on Monday.The Ro
body: A Russian warship, shadowed by a Royal Navy vessel, carried out a live-fire artillery exercise just 46 miles (74km) south of Plymouth on Monday.The Royal Navy monitored the exercise and is continuing to "track the vessel's activity closely", a Ministry of Defence (MoD) spokesperson said.The Russian frigate, Neustrashimy (English: Dauntless), was also being monitored by a French military aircraft which made radio contact with the warship asking it to clarify its intentions, the BBC has been told.The BBC was told that, prior to opening fire, the Russian warship had informed the Royal Navy Offshore Patrol Vessel, HMS Tyne, of its intention to conduct gunnery activity.The Russian warship requested that HMS Tyne reposition to a safer distance, which it did. The Russian live-fire exercise took place in international waters and lasted for 30 minutes.The incident will be a reminder for the new prime minister of increased Russian naval activity near UK waters in recent months. UK defence sources would not comment on whether the incident might have been timed to coincide with the start of Andy Burnham's premiership.Last month, Royal Marine Commandos boarded a Russian "shadow fleet" oil tanker in the English Channel in a six-hour operation which was the first of its kind by UK armed forces.Also in June, a British couple in international waters in the English Channel came across a Russian warship - Admiral Grigorovich - 23 miles off the Isle of Wight.The frigate fired warning shots as Jane and Alan Kelvey sailed their yacht on what Russia's defence ministry said had been a "dangerous approach". However, the couple said they had not been on a "collision course".Russian warships regularly pass through international waters in the Channel, which are separate from UK and French territorial waters.

schema.headline: Russian warship carried out firing exercise off Plymouth coast

title: Will the Tate brothers be extradited from the US to the UK?
description: The CPS said on Sunday that Andrew was being charged with seven further counts of rape, three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexu
body: The CPS said on Sunday that Andrew was being charged with seven further counts of rape, three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation, three counts of assault occasioning actual bodily harm and 19 additional charges for offences relating to indecent images of a child and extreme pornography.Tristan's new charges include two counts of rape, one count of sexual assault and three counts of arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual exploitation.The alleged offending is said to have taken place between July 2010 and August 2017.Andrew now faces a total of 42 charges in the UK and Tristan faces 17 charges, after CPS had pursued other charges last year."These charging decisions followed receipt of a further file of evidence from Bedfordshire Police and bring the total number of alleged victims in this case to seven," Malcolm McHaffie, Head of the Special Crime Division at the CPS, said.The brothers have been based in Romania, where they are also under criminal investigation. In 2024, Bedfordshire Police secured European arrest warrants for the pair to have them returned from Romania when proceedings against them there conclude. They deny all allegations against them. The brothers have been able to travel to the US since travel restrictions against them in Romania were lifted in early 2025.A US Department of Justice spokesperson said the arrests were "pursuant to extradition proceedings".The UK and the US are parties to a treaty that allows the extradition of people in relation to alleged offences punishable under laws in both countries by at least one year in prison.For a UK case, the CPS prepares a request which is certified by a judge. The final request is sent to be considered by the Home Office, which then forwards the extradition request to the relevant state through usual diplomatic channels.The decision to accept the extradition request is taken by the US State Department. The request will then be considered by the US courts, with a final decision made by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio.Appearing in a federal courtroom in Miami on Monday, the pair wore light brown prison uniforms and were handcuffed and shackled at the waist and ankles.They spoke only when asked if they understood their rights to which they replied together "yes your honour".The brothers' attorney, Joe McBride, told reporters after the brief hearing that his clients are innocent."They've never done nothing wrong. They shouldn't be extradited for crimes they didn't commit," McBride said."It's garbage. It's a stitch up job," he added.He compared the charges against the men to the legal proceedings against US President Donald Trump - which he called a "witch hunt" - and referred to the attempted extradition as "political kidnapping".When asked how long the extradition proceedings would take, McBride estimated ahead of the hearing that it would be "around 60 days".Durham Law School Associate Professor Gemma Davies said there was a clause in the US-UK treaty that prevents extradition for political offences, adding lawyers for the Tates could argue the allegations are politically motivated."However, it's very rare extradition between the UK and US is refused on those grounds," Davies said, adding the test would be subject to a high threshold.She said it was "relatively rare" for an extradition request between the two countries to be refused given the strong working relationship between authorities."It's fairly common to extradite between the two, which means it's well traversed from a legal perspective, and we are two countries that have fairly similar legal systems in terms of criminal justice," Davies said.But Bill Bowring, Emeritus Professor of Law at Birkbeck College, University of London, said the ongoing proceedings in Romania were a "major complication".He said he had not "seen a satisfactory answer" as to why the CPS was now pursuing extradition from the US after a Romanian court ruled in 2024 the pair could only be extradited from that country after the conclusion of their separate case there.The BBC has requested comment from the CPS in relation to its position on the Romanian proceedings.Davies said that if the CPS was successful, the extradition could still take months, with any appeal delaying the process by about a year, "if not more".

schema.headline: Will the Tate brothers be extradited from the US to the UK?

title: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping
description: Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off produc
body: Daisy's business goes live from a studio for a minimum of six hours a day with different presenters interacting with shoppers, from showing off products to answering their questions."It's like having 500 people in your shop," she says.There are a host of platforms and marketplaces now offering livestreams connecting sellers and customers, from Instagram Live, YouTube Shopping, to eBay and Amazon Live. They're all different.Live shopping, it seems, is becoming part of everyday consumer behaviour for many.According to new research from retail agency Savvy Marketing, 30% of shoppers surveyed said they'd bought something from a live shopping event."The big retailers have got to grab hold of this," says Catherine Shuttleworth, CEO of Savvy. "It's grown from nothing to a huge thing really quickly. If you haven't got a strategy for live selling, you're going to miss out."TikTok Shop says its UK sales grew by more than 30% year-on-year in June, with live shopping the fastest-growing format.It says it now has more than 6,000 livestreams taking place every day.At an event in the centuries old Covent Garden market, it hosted 20 small businesses with stalls who were also selling live to customers all over the UK.With nothing more than a smartphone, a lighting kit and a fridge full of fresh meat, halal butcher Manny Malik from Wolverhampton had around two hundred people tuning into his session."Last week we did a TikTok live for three hours and we sold £8,000 worth of meat," he says.It would normally take him three to four days to sell this amount in his family shop, Malik Butchers.They're now opening a second store in Stoke. TikTok, he says, is helping to drive his expansion.But there are fees.TikTok Shop UK keeps up to 9p from every £1 sold on the platform, as its standard commission. As a comparison, eBay's marketplace business sellers typically pay between 6.9p and 14.9p depending on the category. Businesses selling on the dedicated live selling format, eBay Live, pay a combined fee of up to 8.42p including payment processing."It's a lot," says Manny."For us in the meat game, there aren't huge margins. However, it's opened up a new platform. We didn't have these sales prior to TikTok. We're not losing money, we've got enough profitability in there," he adds.Buket Sevik, 34, founded her jewellery brand Adorina in February after quitting her 9-5 job as a business manager. Her new business went from 50 orders a month to 500 orders a week since she started live selling on TikTok UK shop in May. She's gone from working from her dining table to live streaming from a small unit, where orders are now being packed."It's crazy," she laughs.Demand is so high, she's trying to hire someone to help.But for Buket, the best thing about livestreaming is the flexibility. "I can go live whenever I want... which means it works absolutely perfectly for me as a mum. I can put my daughter to sleep and I can just hit go live and do some sales."Savvy's latest survey of shoppers also found that 66% of Gen Y and Gen Z shoppers said seeing products sell out or go viral made them more likely to buy.That's something that worries the free debt advice service, Money Wellness, which warns live shopping events are "a recipe for overspending", says external relations manager Rebecca Lamb."Every time you snap up a bargain before it sells out, your brain gets a dopamine hit - the same rush you get from gambling," she says."And because a lot offer one tap payment, there's no card, no basket and no total. Often you've spent money before your brain has caught up," she says.

schema.headline: £100,000 of TikTok sales in a day: The business of live shopping

title: Newcastle United: Why club are changing badge
description: Newcastle United supporters are set to vote on a new crest, with the club poised to change their badge for the first time since 1988.The existing embl
body: Newcastle United supporters are set to vote on a new crest, with the club poised to change their badge for the first time since 1988.The existing emblem is popular with supporters, but Newcastle feel it "struggles to perform in a modern setting"."Its intricacies and irregularities make consistent, high-quality reproduction impossible - from modern digital platforms to broadcast environments and merchandise," the club said.Newcastle's plans to update the crest were first publicly mooted in May 2025.The club have discussed "potential routes forward" with the fan advisory board and surveyed 19,500 members and season ticket holders.Supporters' feedback helped shape the options, which will ultimately be presented on the ballot.A total of 84% of those surveyed made it clear they wanted Newcastle to "honour our heritage" rather than completely overhaul the crest.Supporters also ranked which elements of the current crest they believed were most important, including the castle, seahorses, black and white shield and ribbon.Eligible voters - a group that comprises season ticket holders, members and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's home matches last season - will pick their favoured design during a two-week window in August.BBC Sport asked supporters for their views and one respondent, Ian, suggested voters should have a "none of the above option"."It's hard to say supporters are getting their say when the club came out and said this is happening whether anyone likes it or not," added another, Matt."These new crests represent a simplified, homogenised version of everything that has ramped up post-Covid. "I can already see the final product; a stripped-down version of a crest that is more caricature than representative of anything meaningful."However, Newcastle supporter Phil highlighted how the club "at least seem to be listening to fans instead of barrelling into some soulless, corporate design".Another supporter, Andy, "loves" the existing badge, but also stressed "we have to understand that things change"."Whatever brand you look at, there has been evolution to meet the demands of the modern world," he added. "We should be no different. It seems like they're trying to go about it in the right, respectful way, but we have to let it happen."

schema.headline: Newcastle United: Why club are changing badge

title: Starmer ally takes swipe at Burnham's first big policy
description: Whenever there is a reshuffle at the top of government, it is worth keeping an eye and an ear on the dislodged, demoted and rejected former ministers.
body: Whenever there is a reshuffle at the top of government, it is worth keeping an eye and an ear on the dislodged, demoted and rejected former ministers. What do they choose to do and say next?Andy Burnham has started his first full day in the job with a promise to cut VAT from household electricity bills from 1 October.But already – within hours - he is taking flak from a man who just yesterday was in a senior job in Downing Street working alongside Sir Keir Starmer.On Monday night, the former Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister, Darren Jones, posted a picture on X of him having a beer, external, captioned "thank you and good night. See you September".If that was meant to suggest a vow of silence it only lasted a little over 12 hours, because at breakfast time this morning came another post, this one unaccompanied by any picture but loaded with political bite."Good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills," wrote Jones. "It's a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower. But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget."Ouch - that will sting in Downing Street.And yes, there are four references in the Office for Budget Responsibility's Economic and Fiscal Outlook from November, external of last year that refer to Digital ID and how and where the money will come from to pay for it is not clear.For example, on page 10, it says "the cost of digital ID cards, for which no specific funding has been identified". On Page 172 it talks about "the risks around the implementation of digital ID cards for which no explicit provision has been made for its £0.6bn annual cost".Clearly, this was something of a critique of the funding of the policy Jones was previously an advocate of, so the charge was as relevant to him and Sir Keir Starmer's government about their idea as it is to Andy Burnham and his new idea.But it is Burnham who is now prime minister and has to set out, as he put it in his first speech in Downing Street, "how we pay for" the policies they are advocating.And all this just days after Sir Keir Starmer's successor emphasised the need to end "factionalism" within the Labour Party.At lunchtime, Burnham will gather his cabinet for the first time, after its appointment yesterday became something of a nocturnal affair.At gone 10pm on Monday night, there was still a minibus full of ministerial wannabes inside Downing Street waiting for their new positions to be confirmed.One of the later posts to be filled was that of defence secretary, a role taken by Wes Streeting, whose resignation as health secretary two months ago contributed to the draining of Sir Keir's authority as prime minister.One of the key stories of the reshuffle was a pretty significant cull of the ministers closest too and most loyal to the now former prime minister, Darren Jones among them, but also David Lammy, Peter Kyle, Lord Hermer, Steve Reed and Rachel Reeves – albeit Reeves, I am told, was offered a cabinet post but turned it down.The question, in the coming days, weeks and months is how much do we hear in public from those banished Starmerites, as Andy Burnham's government sets out its stall.I hadn't anticipated it starting so soon.

schema.headline: Starmer ally takes swipe at Burnham's first big policy

title: AFC Bournemouth: Alex Scott rejects new contract with Premier League club
description: Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott has rejected a new contract amid interest from rival Premier League clubs in the England Under-21 international.The
body: Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott has rejected a new contract amid interest from rival Premier League clubs in the England Under-21 international.The Cherries made it a summer priority to tie Scott down to fresh terms, but as things stand there is no agreement.Arsenal, Manchester United, Tottenham, Manchester City and Chelsea have an interest in Scott.BBC Sport reported in June that Arsenal had an approach for Scott turned down by Bournemouth.The 22-year-old, who joined Bournemouth from Bristol City in 2023 for £25m, has two years left on his current deal with the Premier League side.Bournemouth remain steadfast in their position that Scott is not for sale, despite him rejecting the contract offer that had been on the table for at least two months.Bournemouth have insisted publicly, directly to the player and to interested clubs that he will remain on the south coast, even on his current contract.That stance has been consistent and, despite talk of an £80m valuation, they have continued to reject approaches.The south coast club are under no pressure to sell after qualifying for the Europa League and generating significant income through the sales last summer of Dean Huijsen, Milos Kerkez, Illia Zabarnyi and Dango Outtara.They have taken a similar stance with highly rated forward Junior Kroupi, who is attracting interest from Arsenal and Tottenham.Brazil international Rayan is also not expected to leave and has given no indication that he wants to, having only joined the club in January.

schema.headline: AFC Bournemouth: Alex Scott rejects new contract with Premier League club

title: Row escalates in Ukraine over army chief's future
description: Image source, ReutersImage caption, The role of Ukraine's commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrsky, has been part of the country's rumour mill ByPaul Adam
body: Image source, ReutersImage caption, The role of Ukraine's commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrsky, has been part of the country's rumour mill ByPaul AdamsDiplomatic correspondentReporting fromKyivAs discussions continue to resolve the crisis caused by President Zelensky's decision last week to part ways with Ukraine's popular Defence Minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, those organising daily protests in support of the minister have issued a threat."If, by Friday July 24th [commander-in-chief Oleksandr] Syrskyi has not been dismissed and Federov has not been nominated for the post of Minister of Defence," army veteran Dmytro Koziatynsky wrote on social media, "we will begin an open-ended nationwide protest."No protests are currently scheduled before then. Ukraine is waiting, anxiously, for a resolution.Over the weekend, the news was full of reports of meetings and discussions, as Zelensky consulted ministers and generals.Amid a welter of rumours, the General Staff was forced to deny reports that Gen Syrskyi was about to be – or even had already been – sacked. Another report suggested that Fedorov had refused an offer to return to a different position in government.Kyrylo Budanov, head of the president's office, urged Ukrainians to remain calm."The situation surrounding the latest internal changes has caused strong emotions," he wrote on Tuesday.With the noisy calls of angry protesters echoing around the tight confines of Ivana Franka Square, within earshot of the presidential palace, Budanov said the government was taking note."I understand people who express their position…..The position of society has been heard."Image source, EPA/ShutterstockImage caption, Protesters hold signs which say 'Fedorov is the defence minister', 'Don't mess with what works', 'Hands off Fedorov' and 'Bring Fedorov back' in central Kyiv on SaturdayWith large numbers of demonstrators directing their anger towards Gen. Syrskyi – seen by many as representing outdated Soviet-era military doctrines, Budanov, a former head of military intelligence, warned people "not to hate anyone"."The enemy is closely monitoring our every internal dispute and is waiting for us to start fighting among ourselves," he wrote."The work is underway. There will be results."On Monday, the beleaguered but usually silent 60-year-old Gen Syrskyi hit back against his critics, publishing a long defence of his record and urging readers not to focus on his widely-reported rivalry with 35-year-old Mykhailo Fedorov.Despite Zelensky's claim last week that the two men could not even bear to sit in the same room, Syrskyi said it had been a surprise to learn that he had a conflict with the minister."I perceived our relationship as a working one," he wrote in an op-ed for the Militarny website."If I offended anyone, Mykhailo Albertovych [Fedorov], I'm sorry."The general said the issues of military contracts, procurement and mobilisation currently being aired in the press and boiled down to pithy slogans on pieces of cardboard carried aloft by protestors were a great deal more complicated than most people realised."Decisions on which people's lives depend are not made with slogans," he said. "Real work, not the public drama surrounding it, wins the war."Image source, Anadolu via Getty ImagesImage caption, Former defence minister Mykhailo Fedoro, giving a briefing after his dismissal last weekLike the ousted defence minister, the commander-in-chief avoided directly personal attacks, but the object of much of his criticism was pretty clear."Presentations should not turn into self-representations," he wrote, in a thinly veiled swipe at Federov's long, detailed press conference last week."Behind each slide there should be a document, a budget and a procedure," the general went on. "Otherwise, instead of a decision the army gets disappointment."Syrskyi's problem however is that most Ukrainians, especially the younger ones, seem to trust Federov's analysis of what needs to change in order for Ukraine to win the war, not the general's.More than anyone, Federov is seen by many as driving innovation in the military, gradually improving Ukraine's chances of beating back its numerically superior enemy."Right now, the war is changing, so we need new ideas and a new structure," 44-year-old law company employee Tetiana Makarenko told me at one of the protests over the weekend."It's often said that a small Soviet army can't defeat a big Soviet army. It's time to change the mindset and start the new type of war."Federov's six months at the helm in the defence ministry have coincided with a string of military successes for which the minister, not always fairly perhaps, is credited."We had this feeling because we saw the changing situation," Makarenko said. "We had the feeling that we can do this."In his article, Syrskyi hit back at those accusing him of a lack of strategy, or that he had failed to embrace new technology."The strangest reproach that I have heard is that I supposedly do not want to fight with drones," he said, pointing out that he had presided over the creation of an unmanned systems force as a separate branch of the military.Image source, Matthew Goddard/BBCImage caption, A crowd of mostly young people gathered in Kyiv, pictured here, and other Ukrainian cities on Thursday morningBut drones, despite being responsible for the lion's share of Russian casualties, were not enough, he argued."I cannot transform a million-strong army to drones in two months and say: now we fight like this."Syrskyi said he was not at war with the defence ministry but with Russia. But while he mounts his defence against a host of critics, speculation has fallen on who Zelensky might choose to replace him.The candidates include Mykhailo Drapaty, commander of the Joint Forces, Andriy Bileksky, commander of the Third Army Corps, Oleh Apostol, commander of the Airborne Assault Forces and Ihor Obolensky, commander of the National Guard Corps.All have distinguished military records, but crucially all are from a much younger generation of officers. At 46, Biletsky is the oldest while Apostol and Obolensky are both 39.At just 35, the youngest possible candidate is Brig Gen Denys Prokopenko, another National guard commander, whose "Azov" Regiment, while politically controversial for its alleged far-right connections, achieved near mythic status during the bitter siege of the southern port city of Mariupol in 2022.Held as a prisoner of war by Russia following the collapse of the city, Prokopenko returned to Ukraine as part of a prisoner exchange in July 2023, quickly resuming his command despite Russian demands that he remain outside the country until the war was over.Zelensky says he is still working out "what adjustments need to be made to Ukraine's defence strategy" and has thanked everyone "for their openness, honesty and substantive contributions".With Russia expected to resume its attacks on Ukraine's energy infrastructure this autumn, the president says "Ukraine must enter August as strong as possible".As the protests fall silent for now, the country waits for the president to make a series of possibly fateful decisions.

schema.headline: Row escalates in Ukraine over army chief's future

title: Gianni Infantino: La Liga's Javier Tebas says it is Fifa president's 'time to go'
description: La Liga president Javier Tebas has called for Fifa president Gianni Infantino to step down, claiming the world governing body is "destroying the footb
body: La Liga president Javier Tebas has called for Fifa president Gianni Infantino to step down, claiming the world governing body is "destroying the football industry".In an interview with Italian newspaper La Gazzetta dello Sport, external, the head of the Spanish league also criticised World Cup hydration breaks, the final's lengthy half-time interval, the postponement of a suspension for United States striker Folarin Balogun, and the future possibility of a 64-team summer tournament.When asked if Infantino should quit, Tebas said: "In my opinion, yes, I think his time is up."However, he stated that the 56-year-old has the support of national federations, and claimed Fifa's election system is "rotten at its core"."There's no opposition candidate, nobody wants to stand just to lose," Tebas said."He shouldn't stay, but the current state of affairs means he won't leave."The head of Spain's top flight was in the US to see his country win the 2026 World Cup."These past few days here in America, I've heard many people who are against Infantino, who disagree with what he's doing," Tebas said."They say it, but then they do nothing. I don't know what's worse, the silence or the complicity, because those who stay silent are perfectly aware of the damage football is suffering."Infantino is expected to be re-elected for a fourth term at Fifa's March congress, although Tebas believes Infantino could have been brought down as a consequence of his involvement in the case around Balogun.The 25-year-old US player escaped a suspension for his side's last-16 tie with Belgium following an intervention from US President Donald Trump."The suspension of the American player's ban was absolutely serious. They were lucky that Belgium eliminated the USA, because otherwise a case could have arisen that might have cost Infantino his job," Tebas said."Since Belgium won, they managed to bury the matter. But these things are just the tip of the iceberg."European football's governing body Uefa criticised Fifa's decision at the time, calling it "unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable", and the decision was a factor in the organisation's president Aleksander Ceferin not attending the World Cup final.Infantino opened the door to a 64-team global tournament during this year's event, but Tebas argued that "not everything can revolve around the World Cup"."Increasing the number of national teams makes no sense. The football industry isn't just the World Cup," Tebas said."It's the national competitions that sustain this sport. They are destroying the football industry, which generates tens of thousands of jobs, for an event that lasts 40 days and involves a minority of players."Are the best players there? Clearly, but they are also in our leagues. We need fewer international matches and more protection for national football, at all levels. They don't realise they are making irresponsible decisions."Fifa brought in mandatory three-minute hydration breaks for the World Cup, regardless of conditions, for all matches played at stadiums in the United States, Mexico and Canada.The governing body said they were brought in as a commitment to player welfare, but critics have pointed to broadcasters using the breaks as a way to cash in with commercials.Meanwhile pop music giants Madonna, BTS, Shakira and Justin Bieber were among the acts to perform in the World Cup final half-time show, which extended the usual 15-minute half-time break to 27 minutes 22 seconds."Fifa arranges things as it pleases, for what it wants, for its own interests, certainly not for football," said Tebas. "So, if they need a 27-minute half-time break, they'll have it."Hydration breaks are a lie. We have them in La Liga, but only when it's genuinely hot."Here, the pitches in Dallas, Los Angeles, Atlanta had air conditioning; I had to put on a jumper in the stands. It was a lie, a break for advertising."It's proof that we are governed by an institution that does and decides what it wants, when it wants, following only its own interests without considering the rest of football. And in many cases, it acts to the detriment of football."BBC Sport has contacted Fifa for comment.

schema.headline: Gianni Infantino: La Liga's Javier Tebas says it is Fifa president's 'time to go'
